name: Neuschwanstein Castle
shortDescription: Fairy-tale Bavarian palace that inspired Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle.
description: Built by the eccentric King Ludwig II on a rugged hilltop above Hohenschwangau, Neuschwanstein is the archetypal Romantic castle. Its towers, turrets, and lavishly decorated interiors were inspired by Wagnerian operas. After Ludwig's mysterious death in 1886, the castle was opened to the public and became one of the most visited buildings in Europe.

name: Edinburgh Castle
shortDescription: Scotland's most iconic fortress perched on an ancient volcanic plug.
description: Dominating the Edinburgh skyline from Castle Rock, Edinburgh Castle has played a central role in Scottish history for over 900 years. It houses the Scottish Crown Jewels, the Stone of Destiny, and St Margaret's Chapel — the oldest surviving building in Edinburgh. The fortress has been attacked at least 26 times, making it one of the most besieged places in the British Isles.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage (part of Old & New Towns of Edinburgh)

name: Malbork Castle
shortDescription: The world's largest castle by surface area, built by the Teutonic Knights.
description: Constructed on the Nogat River by the Teutonic Knights in 1274, Malbork is the largest castle in the world by land area. This Gothic brick fortress served as the order's headquarters and controlled the grain trade of the Baltic region. Heavily damaged in World War II, it was painstakingly rebuilt and is now a masterpiece of Gothic military architecture.

name: Caernarfon Castle
shortDescription: Edward I's mighty Welsh fortress with distinctive polygonal towers.
description: Built by Edward I of England as part of a string of fortresses to control Wales, Caernarfon is one of the greatest medieval fortifications in Europe. Its distinctive banded stone walls and angular polygonal towers were inspired by the Theodosian Walls of Constantinople. The investiture of the Prince of Wales has taken place here since 1911.

name: Hohensalzburg Fortress
shortDescription: One of Europe's largest and best-preserved medieval castles.
description: Perched on the Festungsberg above Salzburg, Hohensalzburg is one of the largest and best-preserved castles in Europe. Founded in 1077 by Archbishop Gebhard von Helffenstein, the fortress was expanded over centuries by the Prince-Archbishops who ruled Salzburg as an independent state. Its panoramic views of the Alps and city make it Salzburg's top attraction.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Historic Centre of Salzburg)

name: Prague Castle
shortDescription: The largest ancient castle complex in the world by area.
description: Founded in the late 9th century, Prague Castle is officially the largest coherent castle complex in the world, covering over 70,000 square metres. It has served as the seat of Czech kings, Holy Roman Emperors, and now the Czech President. Within its walls stand palaces, churches, gardens, and the famous St. Vitus Cathedral.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Historic Centre of Prague)

name: Alcázar of Segovia
shortDescription: A fairy-tale Spanish fortress shaped like the bow of a ship.
description: Rising dramatically from a rocky crag at the confluence of two rivers, the Alcázar of Segovia is one of the most distinctive medieval castles in Spain. Its ship-prow silhouette and blue-slate roofs are said to have inspired Walt Disney's Cinderella Castle. It served as one of the favourite residences of the monarchs of Castile and later as a state prison and military academy.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Old Town of Segovia)

name: Bran Castle
shortDescription: Transylvania's dramatic clifftop fortress, the 'Dracula Castle'.
description: Perched on a 60-metre-high rock in the Carpathian Mountains, Bran Castle is Romania's most visited castle and is popularly associated with Bram Stoker's Dracula. Built by the Saxons of Kronstadt in 1377 to guard a mountain pass, the castle features multiple towers and turrets. Vlad the Impaler, the historical inspiration for Dracula, may have briefly been imprisoned here.

name: Conwy Castle
shortDescription: Edward I's formidable castle guarding the Conwy estuary.
description: One of Edward I's 'iron ring' of castles built to pacify Wales, Conwy stands alongside the walled town he built around it. The castle's eight massive towers and two barbicans made it virtually impregnable. Together with the town walls, Conwy Castle is considered one of the finest examples of late 13th-century military architecture in Europe.

name: Stirling Castle
shortDescription: The key to Scotland, controlling the main passage into the Highlands.
description: Stirling Castle has been a pivotal strategic stronghold throughout Scottish history due to its position at the crossing of the River Forth. Multiple Scottish monarchs were crowned here, including Mary Queen of Scots. The castle changed hands between Scotland and England at least eight times in the Wars of Scottish Independence, and its Great Hall and Renaissance Royal Palace are among Scotland's most important historic structures.

name: Warwick Castle
shortDescription: A magnificent medieval fortress on the River Avon, home to the Earls of Warwick.
description: Built by William the Conqueror in 1068 on the banks of the River Avon, Warwick Castle was developed over 900 years of British history. The Beauchamp family transformed it into a symbol of chivalric power during the 14th century. Today its Caesar's Tower and Guy's Tower remain two of the finest examples of medieval fortification in England.

name: Château de Chambord
shortDescription: The grandest Loire Valley château with its famous double-helix staircase.
description: Built by Francis I as a hunting lodge and statement of royal power, Chambord is the largest château in the Loire Valley. Its roofline is a forest of towers, turrets, chimneys, and dormers designed in the French Renaissance style, possibly by Leonardo da Vinci. Its central keep features the iconic double-helix staircase where two people ascending and descending never meet.

name: Mont-Saint-Michel
shortDescription: A tidal island abbey-fortress rising dramatically from the sea.
description: Rising from the sea on a cone-shaped island in Normandy, Mont-Saint-Michel is one of France's most recognisable landmarks. The tidal island is crowned by a medieval abbey and fortified village. During the Hundred Years' War, the English besieged it for 30 years without success, and its fortifications were considered impregnable. The surrounding bay has some of the highest tidal ranges in Europe.

name: Windsor Castle
shortDescription: The oldest and largest occupied castle in the world, a royal residence since William the Conqueror.
description: Founded by William the Conqueror after his 1066 conquest of England, Windsor Castle has been a royal residence for nearly 1,000 years and is the longest-occupied palace in Europe. It covers nearly 5 hectares and contains over 1,000 rooms. The Round Tower dominates the skyline, and St George's Chapel within its walls is one of the finest examples of English Gothic architecture.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Historic Town of Windsor)

name: Krak des Chevaliers
shortDescription: The finest surviving example of a Crusader fortress.
description: Considered one of the most important preserved medieval castles in the world, Krak des Chevaliers was held by the Knights Hospitaller from 1142 to 1271. Its concentric walls, towers, and massive sloping glacis made it virtually impregnable — T.E. Lawrence called it 'perhaps the best preserved and most admirable castle in the world.' It was captured peacefully by Mamluk Sultan Baybars through a forged letter.
